scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The speech 'Against Conon' was delivered in the Athenian legal system regarding the liturgy of the trierarchy.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'dikastai' (jury) addressed were Athenian citizens participating in the city's democratic judicial system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This oratorical work is contemporary to Demosthenes' career, specifically dated to roughly 341 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'graphe hybreos' was a specific type of public indictment unique to the Athenian legal code.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The public funeral for the war dead (the epitaphios logos) was a quintessentially Athenian state institution.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech is traditionally attributed to Demosthenes following the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The selection of an orator by the city-state for public funerals was a formal Athenian legal and political process.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This rule reflects the contemporary practices of late 4th-century Athens, specifically after Chaeronea.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The concept of autochthony (born from the land) was a core ideological and legal claim for Athenian citizenship.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Demosthenes invokes this contemporary Athenian civic myth in his 338 BCE eulogy.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Parrhesia (freedom of speech) was an institutionalized right specific to the Athenian democratic polity.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The author refers to the ongoing democratic norms of his own city in 338 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Athenian state legally committed to the maintenance of war orphans (the 'orphanoi') as part of its military-civic contract.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The custom was an active feature of Athenian law when the speech was delivered in 338 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: State-funded sacrifices and funeral games for the war dead were established honors in Classical Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The author describes the existing state honors provided by Athens in 338 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Restrictions on specific athletic events like the apobates race to citizens only were specific to Athenian festivals like the Panathenaea.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This work is traditionally attributed to Demosthenes' mid-career (approx. 350 BCE) describing contemporary social norms.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The participation of metics (aliens) and slaves in certain games was part of the inclusive nature of many Athenian religious festivals.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The expectation and eligibility of citizens to hold administrative office was fundamental to the Athenian democratic structure.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isaeus specialized in inheritance cases within the Athenian legal system, where dowry was a central legal requirement.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Athenian law required the return of the dowry to the woman's guardian upon the dissolution of a marriage.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This describes a contemporary legal transaction in Athens circa 389 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Solonic law in Athens allowed childless men to adopt an heir to perpetuate their oikos.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The orator cites the active laws governing inheritance and adoption in 389 BCE Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Athenian 'nomoi' specified that a person must be of sound mind and not influenced by women for a legal act like adoption to be valid.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This reflects the specific legal standards for testamentary capacity in the 4th-century BCE Athenian courts.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Valid adoption in Athens required formal registration with the adopter's phratry and deme.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The procedure described was the standard contemporary civic requirement in 389 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The right to adopt is framed specifically within the context of Athenian religious duties and family continuation.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speaker is using the current laws of Athens to argue his case in 389 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Inheritance rights for adopted sons were a core feature of Athenian family and property law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The argument rests on 4th-century BCE Athenian statutes regarding the status of adopted heirs.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The gymnasiarchy was a local liturgy within the Athenian demes, performed by wealthier citizens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The text refers to public services performed near the time of the trial in the early 4th century BCE.
